response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental mismatch between your .NET 7 project configuration and the WPF libraries it is trying to load, which are traditionally associated with the older .NET Framework ecosystem. When you attempt to reference WPF assemblies in a modern .NET 7 project without the correct target framework or SDK, the runtime often defaults to looking for the legacy .NET Framework version 4.0 of PresentationFramework, leading to the specific file not found exception you see.

To resolve this issue while maintaining your cross-platform strategy, you must ensure that the WPF-specific code is isolated in a separate project that explicitly targets .NET 7 with the Windows desktop workload installed. This means your main cross-platform library should remain a standard class library or console app that targets multiple frameworks, while the UI layer on Windows becomes a distinct executable or class library that specifically targets `net7.0-windows`, allowing the build system to correctly resolve the modern WPF dependencies.

cousin projects in your solution structure should be organized so that the shared logic resides in a framework-agnostic class library that neither directly imports WPF nor Cocoa, but instead defines interfaces for the UI interactions. This architectural pattern allows the Windows-specific project to implement these interfaces using WPF controls, while the macOS project implements the same interfaces using Cocoa, ensuring that the shared code never attempts to load PresentationFramework when running on a Mac.

You need to configure your main application to dynamically load the appropriate UI assembly at runtime based on the detected operating system, rather than having a static reference to the WPF project in the global dependencies. By using reflection or a plugin architecture, the .NET 7 host can load the WPF assembly only when the `RuntimeInformation.IsOSPlatform(OSPlatform.Windows)` check returns true, effectively preventing the runtime from searching for the missing .NET Framework assembly on macOS or in the wrong context on Windows.

Finally, verify that your project file for the Windows UI component includes the necessary `<UseWPF>true</UseWPF>` property and that you have the Windows SDK installed on your development machine to ensure all necessary native dependencies are present. This setup guarantees that the modern WPF assemblies are correctly bundled and referenced for the Windows build, eliminating the version conflict and allowing your application to run seamlessly across both platforms without downgrading your core project to .NET Framework.
